questions.question: What is the primary cause of this thread starvation issue under virtual threads?
options: A) The JVM fails to yield the virtual thread because executing a synchronized block pins the virtual thread to its carrier thread, blocking the carrier thread from executing other virtual threads during the blocking I/O operation.
B) The default carrier ForkJoinPool is limited to a single worker thread when synchronized blocks are detected by the class loader.
C) Virtual threads cannot perform network I/O operations inside synchronized blocks, throwing an uncatchable IllegalStateException that halts the carrier pool.
D) The Spring dependency injection mechanism forces the synchronized block to execute on the main platform thread instead of the virtual thread task scheduler.
questions.correct_answer_text: A) The JVM fails to yield the virtual thread because executing a synchronized block pins the virtual thread to its carrier thread, blocking the carrier thread from executing other virtual threads during the blocking I/O operation.
questions.explanation: In Java 21, when a virtual thread runs inside a `synchronized` block or method (or calls a native method), it 'pins' its carrier platform thread. During a pinning state, if the virtual thread performs a blocking I/O operation, the carrier thread is blocked as well, instead of being released to run other virtual threads. This defeats the cooperative scheduling of virtual threads and leads to carrier thread starvation. To resolve this, developers should replace `synchronized` blocks with `ReentrantLock`, which allows virtual threads to unmount gracefully during blocking operations.

questions.question: Why does the transaction context fail to propagate to the asynchronous task inside the CompletableFuture?
options: A) CompletableFuture transactions automatically roll back unless the nested lambda is annotated with @Commit.

B) Spring's declarative transaction management uses ThreadLocal to store the transaction context (such as the JDBC Connection and Hibernate Session), which does not propagate across thread boundaries to the asynchronous execution thread.
C) The Spring Proxy interceptor rejects any return type of CompletableFuture when transaction boundaries are active.
D) The database connection pool locks the transaction to the current thread ID, throwing an exception if any other thread attempts to commit on that connection.
questions.correct_answer_text: B) Spring's declarative transaction management uses ThreadLocal to store the transaction context (such as the JDBC Connection and Hibernate Session), which does not propagate across thread boundaries to the asynchronous execution thread.
questions.explanation: Spring's transaction propagation relies on a `ThreadLocal` mechanism (via `TransactionSynchronizationManager`) to bind the active database transaction/session to the current executing thread. When `CompletableFuture.runAsync()` is called, the execution shifts to a thread from the common `ForkJoinPool` (or a custom executor). Since `ThreadLocal` variables are not inherited by default across these asynchronously spawned threads, the new thread has no active transaction context, resulting in decoupled database operations, autocommit behavior, or `LazyInitializationException` due to the lack of an active Hibernate Session.

questions.scenario:
You are analyzing a performance bottleneck in an asynchronous pipeline processing user profiles. The application uses a custom configured ThreadPoolTaskExecutor with limited threads to prevent downstream database saturation. However, metrics indicate that during peak hours, CPU usage spikes heavily because tasks in the middle of the pipeline are bypassing your custom pool and executing on the common ForkJoinPool.

java
CompletableFuture.supplyAsync(() -> fetchUserData(userId), customExecutor)
    .thenApplyAsync(user -> enrichUserProfile(user))
    .thenAcceptAsync(profile -> saveProfile(profile), customExecutor);
questions.question: Which of the following explains why tasks in this pipeline are escaping your custom executor?
options: A) The thenApplyAsync stage does not pass the customExecutor as an argument, causing it to fall back to the default ForkJoinPool.commonPool().
B) The thenApplyAsync method automatically inherits the executor of the previous stage only if the previous stage returned a custom subtype of CompletableFuture.
C) The customExecutor rejected the task due to queue saturation, forcing the JVM to dynamically delegate the task to the ForkJoinPool.
D) Virtual threads are enabled, which automatically overrides all custom executors to run on the virtual carrier thread pool instead.
questions.correct_answer_text: A) The thenApplyAsync stage does not pass the customExecutor as an argument, causing it to fall back to the default ForkJoinPool.commonPool().
questions.explanation: In Java's `CompletableFuture` API, chaining methods that end with `Async` (such as `thenApplyAsync`, `thenAcceptAsync`, etc.) have overloaded variants. If an explicit `Executor` is not provided as the second argument, the stage defaults to using the system-wide `ForkJoinPool.commonPool()` (or the thread executor defined by the underlying CompletableFuture implementation), regardless of the executor used by the preceding stage. To maintain resource isolation and prevent thread starvation on the common pool, `customExecutor` must be passed to every async step in the chain.
